title: Lecture Notes on Statistical Physics and Neural Networks
abstract: These lecture notes introduce some topics of classical statistical physics, particularly those that are relevant for neural networks and deep learning. Statistical physics is treated as a branch of probability theory or statistics, with the goal of making concepts such as phase transitions and the renormalization group accessible to readers without prior knowledge of physics. We introduce the Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution and the thermodynamic potentials on a finite configuration space, notably for Ising spins and spin-glass models on a lattice, and then define phase transitions as discontinuities that arise in the limit that the number of lattice points goes to infinity. We further introduce Hopfield networks and Boltzmann machines, which are governed by the same energy function as spin-glass models, and discuss the learning algorithm for restricted Boltzmann machines. In this algorithm hidden neurons are integrated out as in the renormalization group. Finally, modern deep learning is introduced, whose early developments were in part motivated by restricted Boltzmann machines in that they carry many layers of hidden neurons. A description of large language models is given.
summary: These lecture notes provide a mathematical tutorial connecting the physics of phase transitions and renormalization to the mechanics of neural networks. It highlights how concepts like energy landscapes and marginalization explain the behavior of models ranging from spin glasses to Large Language Models.
main_problem: Establishing a pedagogical and mathematical bridge between classical statistical physics (phase transitions, renormalization group) and the framework of modern deep learning and large language models.
main_result: The notes demonstrate that the energy functions of spin-glass models are mathematically identical to Hopfield networks and that the process of integrating out hidden neurons in RBMs is analogous to the Renologization Group process.
method: The author uses probability theory, the transfer matrix method, mean-field approximations, and Renormalization Group (RG) transformations to connect microscopic spin configurations to macroscopic thermodynamic properties and neural network architectures.
model_or_system: The lecture notes cover the Ising model, spin-glass models (e.g., Edwards-Anderson), Hopfield networks, Boltzmann machines, and Transformer architectures used in Large Language Models.
key_observables: Partition function, free energy density, magnetization, two-point correlation functions, temperature, and scaling exponents.
important_parameters: Temperature (beta), coupling constants (J), external magnetic field (B), and scaling parameters for LLMs (number of parameters N, data size D, and compute C).
main_assumptions: Initial treatments assume a finite configuration space; the 2D RG analysis uses a truncated subspace of interactions; and the LLM scaling analysis assumes power-law behavior.
figures_summary: Figure 3 illustrates the RG flow of the 1D Ising model; Figure 4 shows a 2D lattice structure; and Figure 7 displays the power-law scaling of loss relative to parameters, data, and compute.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from basic probability and the Ising model to the Renormalization Group, then to Boltzmann machines and Hopfield networks, and finally to modern deep learning architectures and LLM scaling laws.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
